--- SOURCE 1: An overview on modelling approaches for photochemical and ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124

SUMMARY:
Photo-electrochemical and photocatalytic technologies are promising solutions for solar fuel production and involve a number of physical and chemical phenomena. c r e d,b u l k Surface concentration of species involved in the cathodic reaction This review emphasizes AI’s transformative role in enhancing biomass bioenergy production, positioning it as a critical tool for sustainable energy solutions and future environmental policies to achieve carbon neutrality. Energy, exergy, and enviro-economic analysis was conducted on the PV/T system with each HTFs. The results show that the thermal efficiency of PV/T was enhanced by 5.39, 3.87, and 9.45 % with C-dot, MXene and hybrid NFs, respectively. Solar energy is efficiently converted into electron-hole pairs, which play a pivotal role in water-splitting reactions occurring at the electrode surfaces.

FULL CONTENT:
An overview on modelling approaches for photochemical and photoelectrochemical solar fuels processes and technologies - ScienceDirect

Review

An overview on modelling approaches for photochemical and photoelectrochemical solar fuels processes and technologies
=============================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Gabriele Falciani, Eliodoro Chiavazzo

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.enconman.2023.117366](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.enconman.2023.117366 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0196890423007124&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Continuum and kinetic models for the description of photocatalytic processes. 
*   •Effect of the nanoscale phenomena on the photocatalytic performance at the macroscale. 
*   •Coupling coarse-grained and continuum models to investigate the reactive interfaces. 

Abstract
--------

Photo-electrochemical and [photocatalytic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/photocatalytic "Learn more about photocatalytic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technologies are promising solutions for solar fuel production and involve a number of physical and chemical phenomena. We provide an overview of numerical and analytical tools to describe such phenomena occurring at disparate time and space scales within devices such as photo-electrochemical cells and photo-chemical reactors. On one hand, chemical phenomena include photo-induced [electron transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electron-transfer "Learn more about electron transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), charge separation, recombination, [equilibrium reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/equilibrium-reaction "Learn more about equilibrium reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") between species in solutions and adsorption reactions. On the other hand, examples of [physical phenomena](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/physical-phenomena "Learn more about physical phenomena from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are the transport of [chemical species](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/chemical-specie "Learn more about chemical species from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or self-assembling of molecular structures. In this respect, we critically review [macroscale](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/macroscale "Learn more about macroscale from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[continuum models](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/continuum-model "Learn more about continuum models from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for transport phenomena combined with kinetic descriptions including their possible coupling with models at even lower scales. We specifically focus on atomistic and coarse-grained models able to represent the local environment of the reactive interfaces such as [photoelectrodes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/photoelectrode "Learn more about photoelectrodes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or supra-molecular assemblies. The critical role of the latter structures on photochemical conversion is highlighted: Therefore, morphological structure of self-assemblies, such as [micelles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/micelle "Learn more about micelles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and monolayers, in solution and at the solid–liquid or gas–liquid interfaces are also discussed. Finally, important scientific gaps are identified and possible perspectives for future research outlooked.

1. Solar fuel production processes
----------------------------------

Global population growth and rising energy demand pose significant challenges to a sustainable future [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b1). The use of [fossil fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/fossil-fuel "Learn more about fossil fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the emission of greenhouse gases, particularly [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), into the atmosphere are major contributors to [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b2). The world energy demand is expected to double by 2050 and triple by the end of the twenty-first century [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b3). Both policymakers and the general public are concerned about the depletion of fossil fuels, the environmental consequences of their use and importantly possible mitigations actions [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b4). Hence, a significant shift in the global energy consumption mix is needed in the upcoming years, inducing scientists and engineers to search for zero-carbon, [renewable energy sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/renewable-energy-source "Learn more about renewable energy sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as wind, solar, hydropower, and geothermal to fulfill this energy demand [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b6). [Synthetic fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/synthetic-fuel "Learn more about Synthetic fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") generated from renewable sources can be one of the possible solutions to decrease the impact of our society on the environment [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b7), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b8). An obvious choice to produce synthetic fuels is to exploit solar energy, which has an enormous potential as a clean, abundant, and economical energy source [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b9), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b11). The generated solar fuel would be an energy vector able to overcome the main problem related to solar energy, namely its intermittency [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b12). Hydrogen is one of the possible solar fuels and can be obtained from water splitting reducing protons while oxidizing water [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b14), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b15). It has the highest [energy density](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/energy-density "Learn more about energy density from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") among the fuels (120 MJ/kg) and when burnt generates H 2 O as byproduct. Moreover, it can be used in existing [internal combustion engines](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/internal-combustion-engine "Learn more about internal combustion engines from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), fuel cells and [gas turbines](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/gas-turbine "Learn more about gas turbines from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b16). However, being the lightest element, both its storage and distribution are challenging [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b17). As a complementary approach, researchers have focused their efforts in using CO 2 as a potential resource to be converted in [carbon based fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/carbon-based-fuel "Learn more about carbon based fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), from which also carbon based chemicals can be obtained [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b7), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b18). Indeed, carbon, capture, storage and utilization technologies are receiving an increasing attention [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b19).

CO 2 is a thermodynamically stable and chemically inert molecule. This makes the reduction of CO 2 a challenging process due to both thermodynamics and kinetics [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b20). Multiple electron (and proton) transfers are required in order to reduce CO 2 to valuable products through several radical intermediates [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b21). A possible first step in the [carbon dioxide reduction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/carbon-dioxide-reduction "Learn more about carbon dioxide reduction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") process is the addition of a single electron to the CO 2 molecule which causes a bending of the linear molecular structure owing to the repulsion of the acquired electron located on the carbon atom and the [free electron](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/free-electron "Learn more about free electron from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") pairs on the two oxygen atoms. This configuration makes the [anion radical](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/radical-anion "Learn more about anion radical from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") CO 2−. highly [energetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/energetics "Learn more about energetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Indeed, the single-electron reduction of CO 2 has a strongly negative electrochemical potential (−1.90 V vs [Normal Hydrogen Electrode](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/normal-hydrogen-electrode "Learn more about Normal Hydrogen Electrode from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") — NHE) [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b22). Other proposed reaction mechanisms bypass the formation of the radical CO 2−. by involving a series of multiple proton-coupled [electron transfers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electron-transport "Learn more about electron transfers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PCET) thus requiring less electrochemical potential [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b23), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b24). CO 2 adsorption on a [semiconductor surface](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/semiconductor-surface "Learn more about semiconductor surface from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is one method of activating the otherwise inactive molecule for reduction [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b22). In general, the surface atoms of the [photocatalyst](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photocatalysts "Learn more about photocatalyst from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") coordinate with the carbon or the oxygen atoms of the CO 2 molecule as shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#fig1). The different binding modes partly determine the reaction pathways [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b21). Depending on the reaction pathway and the number of electron transferred, it is possible to obtain multiple different products such as [carbon monoxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-monoxide "Learn more about carbon monoxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [formic acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/formic-acid "Learn more about formic acid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), formaldehyde, methanol and methane [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b25). Among many products obtainable from CO 2, CO is a gaseous compound that requires the transfer of only two electrons, and thus it is a kinetically favorable choice as compared for example to methane, which requires the transfer of eight electrons to form one molecule as shown in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#tbl1). Moreover, a mixture of CO and H 2, coming from the proton reduction of water, can be employed in chemical reactors to produce more complex hydrocarbons through the Fischer–Tropsch process [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b26), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196890423007124#b27). The obtained energy rich fuels such as methanol or methane, can be already used in the current infrastructure [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/

enriched_points.expanded_content: Various solar fuel technologies are being implemented globally, including hydrogen production via photoelectrochemical cells, demonstrating effective use of solar energy in sustainable fuel generation.
Solar fuel projects stimulate job creation and economic growth, particularly in rural areas, by promoting energy independence and reducing reliance on fossil fuels.
Recent advancements in photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical processes significantly enhance conversion efficiency, enabling the practical application of solar energy for fuel production.
Experiences from pilot projects highlight the importance of interdisciplinary research, collaboration, and adaptive strategies to address challenges in solar fuel technologies.
Ongoing research in artificial photosynthesis and carbon capture technologies presents opportunities for innovative solutions, potentially transforming solar fuels into mainstream energy sources.
